data.title: Saying Goodbye to the Old Year and Welcoming the New in 2026: Giving AI a Soul and Web3 a Root
data.description:
Author: Zhang Feng

Only by drawing strength from traditional wisdom and remaining clear-headed amidst cutting-edge trends can we find our place in this reconstruction.As the clock steadily ticks into the Year of the Horse in 2026, we stand at a remarkable historical juncture. Before us lies a magnificent wave of technological advancement, behind us lies a wellspring of wisdom accumulated over millennia.

This is the best of times, and also the era that most desperately needs strength.

This is the best of times, and also the era that most desperately needs strength.

The world is being restructured at a dazzling pace, and each of our mindsets, knowledge structures, and survival strategies—each of our operating systems—must also be restructured accordingly. I. The Surroundings and the World: What Exactly Is Happening? We are witnessing a fundamental transformation in the way information is processed. If the past few decades saw informatization confined to data collection and storage, today we have fully entered the "industrialized production era" of information processing. Artificial intelligence is no longer mysterious code in laboratories, but permeates every stage of production like electricity. In 2026, the development of AI will exhibit several distinct characteristics. First, there's the large-scale deployment of "multi-agent" systems. As predicted by the industry, this year will be the "first year of multi-agent deployment." Complex workflows that previously required a team of ten—from market planning to human resource selection—can now be completed by a single human employee directing a group of "silicon-based engineers." This is not only an improvement in efficiency, but also a fundamental restructuring of enterprise organizational structures. Second, there's the penetration of AI capabilities into practical applications. In manufacturing workshops, deep learning models no longer rely on the cloud but run directly on edge processors. Millisecond-level decision-making and localized processing of sensitive data enable the true realization of intelligent industrial production. Quality inspection, predictive maintenance, and dynamic energy management—once fragmented processes—are being rewoven by AI into an adaptive production system. AI is reshaping every industry. It is no longer just an efficiency-enhancing tool but has become the core engine for reshaping business processes. Just like the internet in its early days, companies that don't embrace AI today are losing their ticket to participate in future competition. However, the frenzy of computing power has also encountered the boundaries of physics. The miniaturization of transistors is approaching its limits, the power consumption wall is becoming increasingly prominent, and the growth curve of classical computing architectures is beginning to slow down. At this time, quantum technology is rising on the distant horizon, foreshadowing another reconstruction of the underlying logic of technology. Although fault-tolerant quantum computing is still some time away, the path to "quantum-enhanced intelligence" is clear: AI needs quantum computing to overcome computing power bottlenecks, and quantum computing also needs AI to optimize error correction and control. When the growth of computing power changes from linear to exponential, the entire industry's rules will be rewritten. In the financial and asset sectors, the Web3 philosophy is returning in a more pragmatic way. The tokenization of Real-World Assets (RWAs) has become a focus, transforming green and physical assets into tradable digital assets through blockchain technology, creating a new narrative in financial centers like Dubai and Hong Kong. However, the regulatory framework is also rapidly improving. In early 2026, eight Chinese ministries jointly issued a document clarifying the regulatory bottom line for virtual currencies and RWA-related activities, emphasizing the principle of "same business, same risks, same rules." This signifies that Web3 development is moving away from its period of unregulated growth and entering a new stage of compliance and institutionalization. The world is changing in multiple dimensions: Rules and regulations are changing; regulation is shifting from reactive to proactive. Infrastructure is changing; computing power is moving from centralized to edge computing, from classical to quantum computing. Trading models are changing; the form and circulation of assets are being redefined. Faced with such changes, where do we go from here? II. Where Do Wisdom and Strength Come From? The more we are in the midst of great change, the more we must look back at our past. The source of wisdom and strength lies not only in old books or entirely in code, but also at the intersection of tradition, innovation, and practice. The first source is traditional culture. Chinese philosophy contains rich resources for coping with uncertainty. The *I Ching* says, "When things reach an extreme, they change; change leads to progress; progress leads to longevity," but we must also understand the relationship between "change, immutability, and simplicity." Everything in the world is in flux; this is called change. Behind this change lie unchanging laws; this is called immutability. Understanding these laws simplifies the complex world; this is called simplicity. When facing technological waves, if we only see change and not immutability, we will drift with the tide and blindly chase every trend; if we cling to immutability and refuse change, we will become rigid and conservative, missing opportunities. True wisdom lies in using unchanging fundamental laws to deal with the ever-changing world. The second source is technological innovation. This is the direct driving force behind the progress of the times. From the implementation of edge AI to breakthroughs in quantum computing, from the collaboration of multi-agent systems to the expansion of the digital yuan ecosystem, every technological advancement opens up new possibilities. We must maintain a keen insight into the forefront of technology, not out of anxiety, but out of responsibility—understanding the ongoing changes is essential to becoming participants in these changes, not bystanders. The third source is learning by doing. Wang Yangming's concept of "unity of knowledge and action," in today's context, means iterating through practice and gaining understanding through action. Whether it's the implementation of AI applications or the exploration of RWA compliance paths, no one has a ready-made standard answer. True ability is honed in specific projects and accumulated through trial and error. Don't wait for a perfect theory before starting; learn to swim by swimming. III. Direction and Strategy: How to Bid Farewell to the Old and Embrace the New? Give AI a Soul, Give Web3 a Root. "Give AI a soul" means algorithms need personalized warmth and creativity to create equal opportunities for innovation for all. We need to infuse it with a cultural and ethical "soul," making it not just a tool, but a wise partner that resonates with humanity, and a sustainable, future-oriented infrastructure. "Give Web3 a Root" means the decentralized digital world needs to build a solid foundation of trust. In the torrent of Web3, while information and assets flow freely, they are easily tainted by nihilism and speculation. This "root" is a return to the original intention of using technology for good, using reliable digital identity and rights confirmation mechanisms to allow the virtual ecosystem to take root in real social value and humanistic soil. When AI has a soul and Web3 has roots, the digital age can possess both the depth of wisdom and the thickness of trust. Facing an era of reconstruction, mindset determines our state. As individuals, we can extract three aspects of self-cultivation from Chinese philosophy: grasping the world through the principles of "unchanging, changing, and simple." This requires us to embrace change while adhering to principles. Embracing change means remaining open to new things, critical of old models, and daring to break out of our comfort zones; adhering to principles means having a moral compass, knowing what the inviolable bottom line is, and what values ​​must be upheld. What changes are technologies, tools, and business models; what remains unchanged is the commitment to integrity, the reverence for professionalism, and the belief in long-termism. When we can reduce complex phenomena to simple laws, the world becomes less anxiety-inducing. Grasp the direction with the principle of "attaining utmost emptiness and maintaining steadfast stillness." This is an era of information overload and noise. Countless news items, opinions, and trends vie for our attention every day, easily making our hearts "too full and too anxious." Lao Tzu reminds us to clear our minds of distractions and return to inner peace. "Emptiness" is not emptiness, but the clarity that comes after clearing away excessive desires; "stillness" is not lifelessness, but the inner strength maintained amidst the clamor. Only when the mind is like a clear mirror can we see the true nature of things and maintain clear judgment when everyone is busy and confused. Grasp the path with the principle of "learning and practicing regularly." These opening words of the *Analects* reveal the secret to growth. "Learning" is absorbing new knowledge, understanding new trends, and seeking new skills; "practicing" is putting knowledge into practice, receiving feedback, rehearsing, and internalizing it. Both are indispensable. Learning without practicing is mere theoretical discussion; practicing without learning leads to low-level repetition. Moreover, this process must be "regular"—continuous, evolving, and seizing opportunities. In today's rapidly evolving technological world, continuous learning is not just a slogan, but a fundamental necessity for survival. IV. The Stars and the Ground: Neither Can Be Left Behind Looking ahead to the journey in 2026, Giving AI a soul, giving Web3 a root. The stars and the ground: Neither can be left behind. First, building a one-stop business platform for AI. AI is no longer an option, but a necessity. We are deeply integrating AI into our core business processes, not through piecemeal tool applications, but through systematic platform construction. Drawing inspiration from multi-agent thinking, we are exploring how to connect disparate business processes, allowing AI to handle more repetitive and procedural tasks, freeing up human resources for more creative decision-making. Whether in marketing, service, or internal management, we must consider how to enable "digital employees" to collaborate efficiently with human employees. This process is a top-level management project, requiring a strategic drive to evolve the organization's mindset. Secondly, we are exploring compliant distribution paths for RWA. With the release of the new regulations by eight ministries, RWA's development path in China has become clear: Compliance is a prerequisite, and relying on specific financial infrastructure is a necessary condition. We must closely monitor regulatory developments and conduct in-depth research on the compliance framework for issuing assets overseas based on domestic equity. In cutting-edge fields such as green finance and digital assets, we should make early arrangements and conduct steady pilot programs. Dubai's $186 billion green transformation market is connecting with the regulatory clarity in Asia, which contains huge structural opportunities. What we need to do is uphold integrity and innovate—exploring new paradigms for asset digitization within the scope permitted by the rules. Third, we should deepen the business expansion of the digital yuan. In 2026, the digital yuan officially entered the "2.0 era." The leap from a payment tool to core financial infrastructure is underway. We must grasp the main theme of "steady development," combining it with our own business scenarios to find breakthroughs in areas such as smart contracts, cross-border payments, and supply chain finance. The digital yuan is not only an upgrade of payment channels, but also a catalyst for business model innovation. Whoever can seize the initiative in building the scenario ecosystem will gain the upper hand in the future digital finance landscape. As the Book of Songs says, "Though Zhou is an old state, its mandate is ever new." Our civilization is ancient yet ever-new precisely because it can always remain open amidst change and continuously innovate while inheriting traditions. For us personally, the world, technology, and rules will change in 2026, but our pursuit of value, our reverence for professionalism, and our belief in the future will remain constant. Let us, in this era of reconstruction, bravely reconstruct ourselves. Let us cultivate our inner strength with traditional wisdom, ignite our spirit with technological innovation, and face change with composure. Rather than passively waiting for the future, let us create it ourselves. In 2026, let us start from the heart and move towards the new.

data.title: Vitalik stated that Ethereum is freely available for Nexo's return to the US market.
data.description:
Headline

▌Vitalik: Ethereum Can Be Used Freely by Anyone Without Value Agreement

Ethereum co-founder Vitalik Buterin stated in an article on the X platform that users can freely use Ethereum without agreeing with his views on application models, DeFi, privacy payments, AI, etc. As a decentralized protocol, Ethereum should maintain its permissionless and censorship-resistant attributes, but this does not mean that individuals cannot make value judgments and publicly criticize applications. Neutrality should belong to the protocol layer, while individuals and communities need to clearly express their own principles and build an ecosystem consistent with their values, while accepting that Ethereum will be used in different ways by different philosophies.

▌Nexo Reaches Agreement with Bakkt to Return to the US Market

Crypto lending platform Nexo announced on Monday that it has become the latest cryptocurrency company to return to the US market.

Nexo stated that using Bakkt's digital asset trading infrastructure provides its products with a framework that complies with U.S. law. Bakkt is a digital asset platform founded by the Intercontinental Exchange (ICE), the parent company of the New York Stock Exchange. Previously, in 2023, the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) accused Nexo of failing to register the issuance and sale of its retail crypto asset lending product (called an "Interest-Earning Product," or EIP). Nexo agreed to a cease and desist order but neither admitted nor denied that its EIP was an unregistered security. As of press time, according to CoinGecko data: BTC price is $68,779.04, a 24-hour change of +0.2%; ETH price is $1,999.31, a 24-hour change of +1.9%; BNB price is $626.22, a 24-hour change of +2.0%. SOL price is $86.65, up 1.2% in the last 24 hours; DOGE price is $0.1007, down 1.3% in the last 24 hours; XRP price is $1.49, up 1.0% in the last 24 hours; TRX price is $0.285, up 1.7% in the last 24 hours. WLFI price is $0.1006, a 24-hour change of -0.1%; HYPE price is $31.07, a 24-hour change of +2.9%. Policy: US lawmakers are considering restrictions on data and mining facilities, while the crypto industry explains its concerns are unfounded. Due to energy consumption issues, US lawmakers are considering restrictions on data and mining facilities, while the cryptocurrency industry wants to explain that its concerns about cryptocurrencies are unfounded. Bitcoin mining does indeed consume a significant amount of electricity. However, according to a report by Paradigm (whose portfolio includes mining company Genesis Digital Assets), this business model only works when energy prices are exceptionally low—for example, when energy comes from off-peak renewable energy sources—and when it can be released when the public needs it most. The report refutes widespread claims about the energy consumption and waste emissions of Bitcoin mining, citing data showing that the industry actually consumes only about 0.23% of global energy and accounts for about 0.08% of global carbon emissions. Moreover, miners must operate at a "break-even" price per megawatt-hour to be profitable.

▌Hong Kong regulator adds Victory Fintech to list of licensed trading platforms

The Hong Kong Securities and Futures Commission (SFC) announced on Friday that it has added another company to its list of officially licensed cryptocurrency trading platforms.

Victory Fintech Company Limited has been added to the list of licensed virtual asset trading platforms published on the Hong Kong Securities and Futures Commission (SFC) website, bringing the total number of cryptocurrency and blockchain entities on the list to 12. Victory's addition marks the first time the SFC has approved the establishment of a cryptocurrency trading platform in Hong Kong since June 2025. The Netherlands has approved a major reform of its current Category III tax system, which will tax "real gains" on savings and investments, including annual changes in the value of liquid assets such as Bitcoin, at a uniform rate of 36%. The proposal is tentatively scheduled to take effect on January 1, 2028, pending Senate approval. This move signifies a fundamental shift in how European governments treat digital assets: from taxing sales to taxing holdings.

Blockchain Applications

▌OKX Obtains European Payment License to Expand Stablecoin and Cryptocurrency Card Business

OKX has obtained a Payment Institution (PI) license in Malta, complying with EU regulatory requirements that will take effect in March. This license allows OKX to continue providing stablecoin-related payment services throughout the EU, fully compliant with the Crypto Asset Markets (MiCA) Regulation and the Second Payment Services Directive (PSD2). Erald Ghoos, CEO of OKX Europe, stated, "We recently launched real-world payment products, including OKX Pay and OKX Card, bringing stablecoins into everyday life. Obtaining the payment institution license ensures that these products operate in full compliance." At the end of last month, OKX partnered with Mastercard to launch a cryptocurrency payment card in Europe, and its venture capital arm, OKX Ventures, recently invested in the stablecoin issuance platform STBL.

▌deBridge Launches MCP Enabling AI Agents to Execute Non-Custodial Cross-Chain Transactions

Cross-chain protocol deBridge announced the launch of its Model Context Protocol (MCP), allowing AI agents and developer tools to execute exchanges, bridges, and multi-step transactions between the EVM chain and Solana. This release follows the launch of deBridge Bundles, an intent-based execution model designed to eliminate chain-level complexity.

▌Musk: My “Net Worth” Comes Almost Entirely from My Shares in Tesla and SpaceX

In response to reports that he is about to become a trillionaire, Musk stated, “My ‘net worth’ comes almost entirely from my shares in Tesla and SpaceX. I have less than 0.1% in cash. All employees at Tesla and SpaceX hold stock/options, and over 80% of Tesla's shares are held by retail investors and index/pension funds, so any growth in value benefits these people.”

▌X Platform Gradually Recovering from Outage

The X platform (Twitter) has gradually recovered from the outage.

Harvard University Establishes Ethereum ETF Position for the First Time and Reduces Bitcoin ETF Holdings by Approximately 21% in Q4 2025

A recent filing by Harvard Management Company, the fund manager of Harvard University, shows that it reduced its Bitcoin ETF holdings by approximately 21% in the fourth quarter of 2025, while simultaneously establishing an Ethereum ETF position for the first time, bringing its total exposure to the two major crypto assets to approximately $352.6 million. As of December 31, 2025, Harvard held approximately 5.35 million shares of BlackRock's IBIT, valued at $265.8 million, a decrease of approximately 1.48 million shares from the previous quarter's 6.81 million shares. At the same time, it established a new position in the iShares Ethereum Trust, holding approximately 3.87 million shares, valued at approximately $86.8 million, marking the fund's first publicly disclosed allocation to Ethereum-related products.

... Despite some reductions, Bitcoin remains Harvard's largest publicly disclosed equity holding, exceeding its holdings in Alphabet, Microsoft, and Amazon.

▌ECB's Nagel: Euro Stablecoins Facilitate Cheap Cross-Border Payments

ECB Governing Council member Nagel stated that euro-denominated stablecoins can facilitate cheap international transfers, complementing the ECB's efforts to promote a digital euro. He pointed out that Europe must be more independent in its payment system and emphasized the importance of creating a digital euro. Nagel mentioned that euro-denominated stablecoins can be used by individuals and businesses for low-cost cross-border payments, demonstrating the ECB's increasingly open attitude towards this area.

...

▌Wintermute has launched institutional OTC trading for its tokenized gold products PAXG and XAUT

Wintermute has launched institutional OTC trading for its tokenized gold products PAXG and XAUT. Wintermute CEO Evgeny Gaevoy predicts that even if the overall market remains sluggish, the industry size could still grow 2.8 times to reach $15 billion by 2026.

▌Charles Schwab is recruiting product managers for stablecoin-related businesses

According to recruitment information on Charles Schwab's official website, the company is recruiting product managers (senior managers) for digital assets and stablecoin-related businesses.

It is reported that its digital asset business unit is building crypto products for retail clients, covering account opening, fund transfers, crypto trading, and on-chain transfers, and will focus on the strategy, implementation, and user experience optimization of stablecoin products. According to SaniExp data, 1,871 institutions have submitted 13F reports, disclosing a total of $18.37 billion in Bitcoin ETF holdings. Of the total Bitcoin supply of 21 million, over 95% has been mined, leaving only 1,009,606 Bitcoins to be mined. Metaplanet's Annual Financial Report: $665.8 Million Loss on Bitcoin Holdings, But Balance Sheet Remains "Solid" Metaplanet, a Bitcoin treasury company, released its fiscal year 2025 financial report on Monday. As of December 31, the company recorded a net loss of 95 billion yen (US$619 million), a reversal from the net profit of 4.44 billion yen (US$28.9 million) in fiscal year 2024. The report shows that the loss was mainly due to a 102.2 billion yen (US$665.8 million) valuation loss on its Bitcoin holdings. The company classified this loss as a non-operating expense, stating that it had no impact on cash flow or operating activities. Despite the volatile net profit, the company emphasized the resilience of its capital structure. Metaplanet stated that its balance sheet remains "robust," fully covering its liabilities and preferred stock even with an 86% drop in Bitcoin prices, thanks to its high equity ratio of 90.7%. As of December 31, the company reported liabilities of ¥46.7 billion (US$304.2 million), net assets of ¥458.5 billion (US$2.99 ​​billion), and Bitcoin holdings valued at ¥481.5 billion (US$3.1 billion). The filing shows that in terms of operations, Metaplanet's revenue for fiscal year 2025 reached ¥8.91 billion (US$58 million), a 738% increase from ¥1.06 billion (US$6.9 million) in the previous year; meanwhile, operating profit surged from ¥350 million (US$2.28 million) to ¥6.29 billion (US$41 million), an increase of 1695%. The company stated that its Bitcoin-related business generated ¥8.47 billion (US$55.2 million) in revenue and ¥7.19 billion (US$46.8 million) in operating income, with this growth primarily driven by premium income from Bitcoin options trading.

▌Grayscale: Applies to Convert Aave Trust into ETF

Crypto asset management company Grayscale has filed an S-1 registration statement with the SEC, planning to convert its Aave Trust into a Grayscale Aave Trust ETF. The fund is planned to be listed on the NYSE Arca under the ticker symbol "GAVE," with a management fee of 2.5%, and Coinbase will act as its custodian and prime broker. Previously, Bitwise also applied to launch a similar Aave ETF.

YZi Labs Submits Revised Request for Preliminary Consent to SEC to Expand CEA Industries Board of Directors According to official sources, YZi Labs announced that it has submitted a revised request for preliminary consent to the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC). The document is currently under review. This request aims to expand the company's board of directors and elect nominated director candidates to the board through written consent from CEA Industries shareholders. World Liberty Finance, Trump Family Crypto Project, Deposits 235 Million WLFI Tokens to Binance According to market sources, World Liberty Finance, a Trump family crypto project, has deposited 235 million WLFI tokens, worth approximately $24.13 million, into Binance. Upbit will launch Bittensor (TAO) spot trading. Upbit will launch Bittensor (TAO) markets in Korean Won, BTC, and USDT. Important Economic News: Trump: I will be indirectly involved in Iran negotiations; Iran wants a deal. **Market Sentiment Towards the Dollar Falls to 14-Year Low, Short Positions Hit 2012 High** According to Bank of America's latest foreign exchange and interest rate sentiment survey, market sentiment towards the dollar fell to its most negative level in 14 years in February. Currently, short positions in the dollar have reached their highest level since January 2012. **The Probability of the Fed Maintaining Interest Rates in March is 92.2%** According to CME's "FedWatch": the probability of the Fed cutting interest rates by 25 basis points by March is 7.8%, and the probability of maintaining interest rates is 92.2%. The probability of the Fed cumulatively cutting interest rates by 25 basis points by April is 25.3%, the probability of maintaining interest rates is 73.1%, and the probability of cumulatively cutting interest rates by 50 basis points is 1.6%. The probability of a cumulative 25 basis point rate cut by June is 52.6%.

Golden Encyclopedia

▌What are Long and Short Positions?

Long and short positions represent opposing strategies used by investors and traders to anticipate price movements of an asset under consideration. In the cryptocurrency space, going long and short still apply to concepts from traditional financial markets. To profit from a rise in cryptocurrency prices, going long means buying it and expecting its value to increase over time. In contrast, going short in the cryptocurrency market means selling a cryptocurrency that you don't actually own in anticipation of a price drop, then buying it back at a cheaper cost to close the position and profit from the price decline. Cryptocurrency traders and investors use these strategies to navigate the high volatility and speculative nature of digital assets and to capitalize on opportunities in both bullish and bearish market conditions.

In cryptocurrency trading, a long position begins by buying an asset in the hope that its price will rise, while a short position begins by disposing of an asset (usually borrowed) in the hope that its price will fall. Closing a position means buying the asset at a lower price to gain profit, while exiting a long position means selling the asset at a higher price to lock in profits. Entry and exit points are crucial for the successful implementation of these strategies. Cryptocurrency long positions have the potential to generate substantial profits through price appreciation, but they also come with significant risks of market volatility and potential losses. Despite these risks, cryptocurrency long positions have the potential to generate significant returns. The opportunity to profit from price increases is the primary benefit. Short cryptocurrency positions can be rewarding by betting on price declines, but they also carry significant risks due to market volatility, unlimited potential losses, and unpredictable price increases. Shorting cryptocurrencies requires precise timing, meticulous risk management, and continuous market monitoring to successfully navigate inherent volatility and maximize potential gains while limiting losses.

data.title: Which sectors performed best during the Spring Festival? A review of BTC and A-share data during the Spring Festival of 2011.
data.description:
Author: Biteye Source: X, @BiteyeCN

On New Year's Eve, the market presented a highly controversial picture: on one hand, the A-share market was booming with pre-holiday sentiment and frequent policy benefits; on the other hand, the crypto market was stuck in high-level fluctuations, even breaking the "ironclad rule of history."

Various jokes and discussions about "whether to sell crypto and buy stocks" abound. Faced with these two markets with drastically different characteristics, how should investors choose? Since we're here during the New Year, let Biteye analyze the current investment logic for you based on the latest data.

I. Core Conclusion: The Spring Festival Market is Not a "Two-Way" Competition

Before delving into the data, we need to clarify a basic understanding: BTC and A-shares each have their own independent "Spring Festival effect," but simply comparing their gains horizontally is not scientific.

Data as of: 2026-02-14 UTC (based on CoinGecko BTC daily OHLC data and Shanghai Stock Exchange announcements)
Statistical caliber: The closing price of the last trading day before the Spring Festival to the closing price of the first trading day after the holiday volatility (approximately 7-8 calendar days, excluding weekend closures) is uniformly adopted to ensure complete alignment between the A-share and BTC time windows

Key observations:

  • III. Policy Bonus for A-shares

    Many people believe that the cryptocurrency market is currently in a bear market, while the global stock market is booming. Even A-shares are being stimulated by policies to beat Ethereum. In addition, the RMB exchange rate is strengthening, so it seems that "withdrawing cryptocurrency and investing in stocks" is a wise move. We will compare them from three dimensions:

    First, yield and volatility. A-shares are a typical policy-driven market. Under strong fiscal stimulus, the A-share sector index recorded significant gains in January. The STAR Market 50 index saw a maximum monthly gain of 15.81%[3], and trading volume increased significantly. Overall, the market showed a pattern of "stable index rise and widespread stock gains". BTC, on the other hand, is heavily reliant on global liquidity. Although it performed poorly during the Spring Festival, BTC is still in the period of consolidation after the halving. Its underlying logic is the dollar tide, not domestic policies.

    Second, long-term compound interest. Based on a ten-year data comparison from 2016 to 2025, the average annual compound return of Bitcoin is about 70.16%; while the average annual compound return of A-shares, taking the CSI 300 as a reference, is about 2.93% [4]. A-shares win in the short term due to policy bursts, while BTC wins in the long term due to compound interest effect and global liquidity premium.

    Thirdly, there is the exchange rate factor. This is the most critical variable at present. On February 13, 2026, as the central bank allowed the RMB to break through the 6.90 mark before the Spring Festival, the RMB exchange rate against the US dollar hit a new high in nearly three years [5]. This means that holding US dollar assets (such as BTC/USDT) will naturally face exchange rate losses when converted back to RMB. As Sina Finance pointed out, the core driving force of this round of RMB strengthening is "enterprises' concentrated settlement of foreign exchange before the Spring Festival". This not only means exchange rate losses, but also reveals the flow of funds: real enterprises are selling off their US dollar positions (including some USDT off-exchange funds) to meet the RMB payment demand during the Spring Festival. This "draining effect" of liquidity puts natural pressure on USD-denominated assets; while holding RMB assets (A-shares) enjoys the double benefit of asset appreciation and currency appreciation. This indeed provides strong logical support for "short-term switching to A-shares." There's an old Chinese saying, "Those who understand the times are wise." Does it really seem like it's time to switch from the crypto market to A-shares? Fourth, the correlation myth: A-shares and cryptocurrencies represent two different funding logics. For those still holding on to the crypto market, can they maintain their beliefs through the "seesaw effect"? That is, A-shares are currently draining the crypto market, and when A-shares fall, funds will flow back to the crypto market? The reality is not so. First, the two show a weak correlation. A-shares are affected by the monetary policy of the People's Bank of China, while BTC is affected by the Federal Reserve's interest rate decisions. While there is some overlap in their funding pools (Chinese investors), they are two independent systems at the macro level. Secondly, there is a certain degree of "extreme homogeneity." Under extreme risk, they often fluctuate in the same direction. For example, on February 6, 2026, the market experienced a typical "triple whammy" – US stocks, gold, and Bitcoin all plummeted simultaneously. This completely disproves the simple "inverse relationship" logic. Therefore, it's difficult to expect an A-share market crash to save the cryptocurrency market, nor should one blindly increase their Bitcoin holdings simply because of an A-share market correction. A negative correlation only exists under one specific circumstance: when domestic funds engage in cross-market allocation for hedging purposes, but this is limited by capital controls and unlikely to achieve a scale effect. Conclusion: Children make choices, adults want it all. Returning to the initial question: Should one invest in stocks or cryptocurrencies during the Chinese New Year? The answer shouldn't be a gamble of choosing between two options, but rather a strategy based on the Merrill Lynch Investment Clock. It's necessary to consider the current domestic and international situation. Domestically, recovery expectations are strong, and A-share Beta returns are attractive, making it worthwhile to allocate to capture policy dividends. Internationally, inflation expectations persist, the liquidity inflection point has not yet arrived, and BTC, as "digital gold," remains a core tool against fiat currency devaluation. Biteye suggests: Adopt a "dumbbell strategy." Instead of repeatedly switching between two markets, enduring high transaction fees and the psychological torment of missing out, it's better to build a stable portfolio: One end of the dumbbell (stable offense): Actively allocate to A-share ETFs or high-quality blue-chip stocks. "Following the trend of RMB appreciation and policy support, fully capitalize on this bullish market," but also be wary of the central bank's "anti-overshooting" signals and avoid blindly chasing highs. The other end of the dumbbell (high-odds defense): Hold onto your core BTC holdings. Despite significant short-term volatility (such as a 38% drawdown), considering the historical inertia of the halving cycle, a complete liquidation might mean permanently losing your low-priced holdings. The Spring Festival of 2026 may be the most "disjointed" in recent years. The strong return of the RMB corresponds to a brief winter in the crypto market. But remember, cycles may be delayed, but they will not be absent. Stock trading requires following the "weathervane" (policy); cryptocurrency trading requires following the "cycle table" (halving and liquidity). In this volatile market, hold onto your holdings, whether it's the red A-shares or the orange Bitcoin; survival is the key to winning—this is the "New Year's posture" of a mature investor.

data.title: Stop bringing up gold; Bitcoin is not a safe-haven asset.
data.description:
Original Title: When Bitcoin Bottoms Compiled by: Peggy, BlockBeats Editor's Note: When Bitcoin underperforms while gold and US stocks frequently hit new highs, the narrative that "funds are rotating from precious metals to crypto assets" has once again become popular in the market. This article does not follow this intuitive judgment to draw trading conclusions, but instead returns to the data itself, systematically examining whether there is truly a verifiable long-term relationship between Bitcoin and gold.

Through correlation and cointegration analysis, it can be seen that there is no robust mean reversion or "one-sided" structural relationship between Bitcoin and gold. The so-called rotation is more of an ex-post explanation than a repeatable and verifiable market mechanism. Bitcoin is neither a safe-haven metal nor a stock index; it is an independent market with extremely high volatility and an evolving structure.

The article further points out that to determine the bottom of Bitcoin, instead of applying analogies to other assets, it's better to focus on the factors that truly determine the market's direction, such as position sizing, derivatives structure, and sentiment clearing. Historical experience shows that most true bottoms are formed when almost everyone has given up. The following is the original text: The crypto market isn't exactly hot right now. While stock and metal prices are constantly hitting new all-time highs, crypto assets have been on a "painful train" since last October. Recently, the timeline has been filled with the claim that "funds are rotating from precious metals to crypto assets, and it could happen anytime soon." Unfortunately, those who express this view are often notorious "empty talkers" in the industry; their only consistently profitable PnL comes from the monthly interaction revenue they receive on the X platform. I'd like to take some time to analyze whether this so-called "rotation from precious metals to crypto" has any real basis (spoiler alert: it doesn't), and then share some key turning points in the history of the crypto market, and how you can identify these moments. The Relationship Between Bitcoin and Gold First, an obvious problem is that to find a relationship between gold's peak and Bitcoin's performance, gold itself would need to frequently "peak." But in reality, gold hasn't had many true peaks in the past decade.

While it's certainly exhilarating to be "hyped," it's best to have data to support your opinions when sharing them online, lest you sound like a complete idiot. Over the past decade, gold has only experienced three relatively significant corrections: 2018, 2020, and 2022. In other words, only three data points. This alone is enough to make me stop researching further; however, to finish this article, let's take a closer look.

... If you look at the chart above, you'll notice that two of the three gold price peaks actually occurred before Bitcoin's downtrend, in 2018 and 2022. The only time Bitcoin strengthened after a gold pullback was during the typical "risk-on" frenzy of 2020. Over the past decade or so, the overall correlation coefficient between Bitcoin and gold has been close to 0.8, which is not surprising—both markets have been rising in the long run. However, correlation alone doesn't answer the question you're really concerned about. If you want to determine whether there's a relationship of "one rising while the other falls, strength and weakness cyclical, and eventual convergence" between two assets, correlation alone isn't enough; you need to look at cointegration. Cointegration measures whether two assets "rise and fall together" in daily fluctuations.
Cointegration, however, asks a different question: Do these two assets maintain a stable relationship in the long run, and are pulled back together once they deviate?

You can imagine it as two drunk people walking home together:

They might each stagger and wander aimlessly (non-stationary), but if they are tied together by a rope, they cannot go too far apart. That "rope" is the cointegration relationship.

If the narrative of "funds rotating from gold to crypto assets" truly has substance, then you at least need to see a cointegration relationship between Bitcoin and gold—that is, when gold surges while Bitcoin significantly underperforms, there should be some real force in the market pulling them back onto the same long-term trajectory.

Combining the charts above, the data actually conveys the following information: the Engle–Granger cointegration test found no cointegration relationship. The p-value for the full sample is 0.44, far exceeding the typically required significance threshold of 0.05. Looking further at the rolling two-year window, none of the 31 intervals showed a cointegration relationship at the 5% significance level. Furthermore, the price spread residuals themselves are non-stationary. The simpler BTC/gold ratio looks slightly more "optimistic," but not much better. An ADF test on this ratio yielded only a barely stable result (p = 0.034), suggesting a potentially very weak mean-regression characteristic. The problem lies in its half-life of approximately 216 days, or nearly 7 months—absurdly slow, almost entirely drowned out by noise. At current levels, Bitcoin's price is roughly equivalent to 16 ounces of gold, about 11% higher than the historical average of 14.4. The corresponding z-score is -2.62, historically suggesting Bitcoin is "cheaper" relative to gold. But here's the key point: this reading is primarily driven by the recent parabolic rise in gold, rather than by some reliable mean-regression relationship that would bring them back together. In fact, no robust cointegration relationship exists. These are essentially two completely different types of assets: gold is a mature safe-haven asset; Bitcoin, on the other hand, is a highly volatile risk asset that just happens to be trending upwards during the same period. If you have no idea what the above is all about, here's a super-brief statistics crash course: The Engle-Granger test is the standard method for determining cointegration. It first performs a regression on the two assets and then tests whether the regression residuals (i.e., the "price difference" between them) are stationary—whether they fluctuate around a stable mean rather than drifting infinitely. If the residuals are stationary, it indicates that the two assets are cointegrated. The ADF (Augmented Dickey-Fuller) test is used to test whether a time series is stationary. Essentially, it tests whether a "unit root" exists; in simpler terms, whether the series will diverge trending outwards or revert to the mean. A p-value below 0.05 means you can reject the "unit root" hypothesis and consider the series to be stationary, i.e., subject to mean reversion. Half-life describes how quickly mean reversion occurs. If the half-life of a price difference is 30 days, it means that once it widens, about half of the deviation will be corrected within a month. Short half-life = trading value; long half-life = basically useless except for "long-term holding and praying." Ultimately, I've always felt that trying to force a relationship between Bitcoin and any traditional financial asset is absurd. Most of the time, people use this comparison to cater to the narrative that best fits their current stance: today Bitcoin is "digital gold," tomorrow it's "leveraged Nasdaq." In contrast, its correlation with the stock market is much more realistic. Over the past five years, Bitcoin's tops and bottoms have been highly synchronized with the S&P 500 (SPX)—until now: the SPX is still firmly near its all-time high, while Bitcoin has retreated 40% from its peak. This is why you should treat Bitcoin as a separate entity. It's not a metal—no one would consider an asset with an annualized volatility exceeding 50% a safe-haven asset (for comparison, gold's annualized volatility is around 15%, and even that is considered relatively high volatility among "store of value" assets). It's not a stock index either—Bitcoin has no constituent stocks; it's essentially just a piece of code. For years, Bitcoin has been shrouded in various narratives: a payment tool, a store of value, digital gold, a global reserve asset, and so on. These descriptions sound appealing, but the reality is that it's still a relatively young market, and it's difficult to say whether it possesses clear and stable practical uses beyond being a "speculative asset." Ultimately, treating it as a speculative asset isn't inherently problematic; the key is maintaining a clear and realistic understanding of this. Finding the bottom in Bitcoin's price action is extremely difficult—of course, no market is easy, but the problem with Bitcoin is that it has changed so rapidly over the years that its historical patterns have become increasingly irrelevant. Ten years ago, the market structure of gold and the S&P 500 (SPX) wasn't significantly different from what it is now; however, in 2015, one of the main uses of holding Bitcoin was to buy heroin online. This has clearly changed dramatically. Today, market participants are much more "serious," especially after the significant increase in open interest in CME Bitcoin futures and options in 2023, and the launch of Bitcoin ETFs in 2024, with institutional funds formally and on a large scale entering the market. Bitcoin is an extremely volatile market. If there's one conclusion we can say with relatively certainty, it's this: market bottoms are often accompanied by violent overreactions and "stampede-like clearing" in various derivatives. This signal is reflected both in native crypto indicators, such as extreme volatility in open interest and funding rates, and in more institutional indicators, such as options skew and abnormal changes in ETF fund flows. I personally built an indicator that integrates these signals into a composite regime for tracking (please note that this indicator is currently not publicly available, sorry). As can be seen from the chart, the areas marked in red typically correspond to periods of extremely low market sentiment: a continuous decline in open interest, negative funding rates, traders paying excessive premiums for put options, and realized volatility exceeding implied volatility. Meanwhile, the spot-volatility correlation in Bitcoin, while still somewhat chaotic overall, is increasingly exhibiting characteristics similar to stock indices.

Summary

If you came here for "entry points, stop loss, and take profit (Entry / SL / TP)," then I can only say I'm sorry to disappoint you (actually, I'm not that sorry).

The purpose of this analysis is more to clarify a seemingly obvious but often overlooked fact: Bitcoin is an independently operating market.

It may resemble gold at times and stocks at others, but fundamentally, there's no inherent reason to expect them to move in sync over the long term. If you're currently staring at falling prices, trying to predict when the bottom will appear, instead of applying analogies to other assets, focus on the data that truly matters to the market. Look at position structure—it often tells the most authentic and brutal story. Also, don't forget: most true bottoms form when almost everyone has given up.

data.title: Why did a key figure in the Ethereum Foundation leave after only 11 months in office?
data.description:
Author: bootly, BitpushNews

The Ethereum Foundation (EF) is once again at a crossroads of personnel turmoil.

Tomasz Stańczak, co-executive director of the Ethereum Foundation, announced that he will step down at the end of this month. This comes just 11 months after he and Hsiao-Wei Wang jointly succeeded longtime leader Aya Miyaguchi in March of last year, forming a new leadership core.

He will be succeeded by Bastian Aue.

Very little information is publicly available about this person; his X account was registered only eight months ago, and he has almost no public activity. He will continue to co-lead this organization, which controls the core resources and direction of the Ethereum ecosystem, alongside Hsiao-Wei Wang. This seemingly sudden personnel change is actually an inevitable result of the intertwining of internal conflicts, external pressures, and strategic transformation within the Ethereum Foundation. Taking on a critical role: A year of turmoil. To understand Stańczak's departure, we must first return to the context of his appointment. In early 2025, the Ethereum community was in a period of anxiety. At that time, the cryptocurrency market was generally rising after the US election, with Bitcoin repeatedly hitting new highs and competing chains like Solana showing strong momentum, while Ethereum's price performance was relatively weak, making the Ethereum Foundation itself a target of criticism. The criticism was directed at Aya Miyaguchi, the then-executive director. The developer community complained that the foundation was severely disconnected from frontline builders, had conflicting interests in strategic direction, and was not sufficiently promoting Ethereum. Some questioned the foundation's overly "laissez-faire" approach, arguing that its mild stance as a "coordinator" rather than a "leader" was causing Ethereum to lose its first-mover advantage. As the "central government" of Ethereum, the foundation was required not to be passive, but to take a strong stance. Amid this storm of public opinion, Miyaguchi stepped back and joined the board of directors. Stańczak and Wang were thrust into the limelight. Stańczak was not an outsider. He was the founder of Nethermind, a core execution client in the Ethereum ecosystem, playing a crucial role in infrastructure development. He is technically savvy, has entrepreneurial experience, and a deep understanding of the community's pain points. In his own words, the instructions he received upon taking office were clear: "The community is crying out—you're too chaotic, you need to be more centralized and accelerate to cope with this critical period." What has been accomplished in the past year? The combination of Stańczak and Wang has indeed brought about visible changes. First, organizational efficiency. The foundation laid off 19 employees, streamlined its structure, and attempted to shed its bureaucratic image. The strategic focus shifted from Layer 2 back to Layer 1 itself, clearly stating that the priority should be scaling the Ethereum mainnet, rather than letting L2 operate independently. The upgrade pace has significantly accelerated, and the advancement of EIPs has been more decisive than before. Second, a shift in attitude. The foundation began releasing a series of videos on social media, proactively explaining Ethereum's technical roadmap and development direction to the public. This "outreach" approach contrasts with its previously relatively closed and mysterious image. Strategically, Stańczak has spearheaded explorations in several new directions: privacy protection, addressing the threats of quantum computing, and the integration of artificial intelligence with Ethereum. Particularly in the AI ​​field, he explicitly stated that he sees "proxy systems" and "AI-assisted discovery" reshaping the world. Financially, the foundation has begun discussing more transparent budget management and funding allocation strategies, attempting to address external concerns about the efficiency of its treasury usage. Vitalik Buterin's assessment of Stańczak is: "He helped significantly improve the efficiency of multiple departments within the Foundation, making the organization more responsive to the outside world." The Hidden Meaning of the Resignation Announcement: Why Leave in Less Than a Year? Stańczak's resignation announcement was quite candid and somewhat intriguing. He provided several key points: First, he believed that the Ethereum Foundation and the entire ecosystem were "in a healthy state." The time for a handover has come. Secondly, he wants to return to being a "hands-on product builder," focusing on the integration of AI and Ethereum. He says his current mindset is similar to when he founded Nethermind in 2017. Thirdly, and most intriguingly, he stated: "The foundation's leadership is increasingly confident in making their own decisions and controlling more affairs. Over time, my ability to execute independently within the foundation is diminishing. If I continue to stay, by 2026 I will be more about 'waiting to hand over the baton.'" This statement reveals two layers of meaning: first, the new leadership team has developed self-motivation and no longer needs his intervention in everything; second, his actual power space may be shrinking: for someone accustomed to hands-on involvement and with a strong entrepreneurial spirit, this feeling is clearly not a good fit. He also mentioned, "I know that many ideas about agent-based AI may not be mature or even useful right now, but it was this kind of game-like experimentation that defined the innovative spirit of early Ethereum." This statement carries a subtle criticism of the current situation: as organizations become more "mature" and decision-making more "stable," will that spirit of unbridled experimentation be lost? Stańczak's departure, ostensibly a personal choice, reflects the long-standing dilemma faced by the Ethereum Foundation. From its inception, this organization has been in an awkward position. Theoretically, Ethereum is decentralized, and the foundation should not be a central authority issuing commands. However, in reality, it controls a large amount of funding, core developer resources, and ecosystem coordination power, objectively assuming the dual roles of a "central bank" and a "national development and reform commission." This paradox of identity has long put the foundation in a dilemma: doing too much leads to accusations of centralization; doing too little results in criticism of inaction. Miyaguchi's approach, leaning towards a "coordinator" role, resulted in criticism of weakness; Stańczak attempted to shift to an "executor" role, which did improve efficiency, but naturally led to a more centralized distribution of power within the organization. Stańczak's resignation statement precisely exposes this tension: as the organization becomes more efficient and decision-making more decisive, the individual room for maneuver among founding team members is actually compressed. For an ecosystem that needs to balance "decentralization" and "market competition efficiency," such internal friction is almost unavoidable. What kind of person is Bastian Aue, who succeeded Stańczak? Very little publicly available information is available. His own description on X is that he previously handled "difficult-to-quantify but crucial work" at the foundation: assisting management with decision-making, communicating with team leaders, budget considerations, strategic planning, and prioritization. This low-key style contrasts sharply with Stańczak's distinct entrepreneurial spirit. In his acceptance speech, Aue stated, "My decisions are based on principled adherence to certain attributes of what we are building. The foundation's mission is to ensure that truly permissionless infrastructure—at its core, the cypherpunk spirit—can be built." This statement sounds more like the language style of the Miyaguchi era: emphasizing principles, spirit, and coordination rather than dominance. Does this mean the foundation will rebalance its direction, shifting from "aggressive execution" back to "principle-based coordination"? Only time will tell. Ethereum's Confusion Stańczak's departure comes at a crucial juncture for Ethereum, as it discusses a series of major proposals. He revealed that the foundation is about to release several key documents, including a concrete plan for "Lean Ethereum," a future development roadmap, and a DeFi coordination mechanism. The "Lean Ethereum" proposal has been jokingly referred to by some community members as "Ethereum's era of weight reduction"—aiming to simplify the protocol, reduce burdens, and make the mainnet run more efficiently. These directional documents will profoundly influence Ethereum's evolution path over the next few years. Changing the core executive at this time undoubtedly adds uncertainty to the implementation of these proposals. A broader context is that Ethereum is facing challenges from multiple fronts: competition from high-performance chains like Solana, Layer 2 fragmentation, the new narrative window of AI and blockchain integration, and the impact of overall crypto market sentiment fluctuations on ecosystem funding and attention. On the same day Stańczak announced his departure, ETH briefly fell into the $1800 range. If it continues to fall below this level, an awkward fact will emerge: the overall return on holding ETH may be lower than the USD cash interest rate. A more sobering calculation: In January 2018, ETH first reached $1400. Adjusted for US CPI inflation, that $1400 would be equivalent to approximately $1806 by February 2026. In other words, if an investor bought ETH in 2018 and held it naked ever since, never participating in staking, then after eight years, not only would they not have made any money, but they would have even underperformed compared to simply depositing US dollars in a bank to earn interest. For the "ETH defenders" who have held onto their faith all along, the real question might not be "who won the battle of philosophies," but rather: how long can they hold on? The only certainty is that this core organization, which controls one of the most important ecosystems in the crypto world, is still searching for its place in the rapidly changing industry, and this path is destined to be anything but peaceful.
